item.title: Twist in Kamikamica trial – Ravu declines to name colleagues who questioned him about his FICAC case
item.description: Former Cabinet Minister Kalaveti Ravu today declined to identify the parliamentary colleagues he claimed had questioned him about his Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption case, while giving evidence in the trial of former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica. Led in evidence by State prosecutor Josann Pene, Ravu told the Suva Magistrates Court that “most people” had approached him abou
item.content:encoded:
Former Cabinet Minister Kalaveti Ravu today declined to identify the parliamentary colleagues he claimed had questioned him about his Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption case, while giving evidence in the trial of former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica.

Led in evidence by State prosecutor Josann Pene, Ravu told the Suva Magistrates Court that “most people” had approached him about the matter while Parliament was sitting on July 8, 2024.

However, when asked to identify those individuals, Ravu clarified that he was referring to fellow Members of Parliament but said he would not disclose their names.

The proceedings were briefly adjourned for 10 minutes before his testimony resumed.

Ravu is a key prosecution witness in the case against Kamikamica.

During the Commission of Inquiry testimony, Ravu had testified that Manoa Kamikamica, the then Deputy Prime Minister had asked him about his FICAC case.

Kamikamica faces one count of perjury, alleging that he falsely stated under oath during the Commission of Inquiry that he had no knowledge of Barbara Malimali’s appointment before it was made on September 5, 2024.

In the alternative, he is charged with one count of providing false information to a public servant by allegedly giving the same false information to the Commission of Inquiry.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FICAC abandons Christopher Pryde prosecution
item.description: The Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC) has discontinued legal proceedings against Director of Public Prosecutions Christopher Pryde. Senior Prosecutor Lani More filed a nolle prosequi this morning, which was accepted by Magistrate Yogesh Prasad. As a result, the charges against Mr Pryde have been discontinued.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC) has discontinued legal proceedings against Director of Public Prosecutions Christopher Pryde.

Senior Prosecutor Lani More filed a nolle prosequi this morning, which was accepted by Magistrate Yogesh Prasad. As a result, the charges against Mr Pryde have been discontinued.

Mr Pryde faces one count of Abuse of Office, alleging that while serving as the Director of Public Prosecutions, he approved and received four gratuity payments amounting to $138,445.05 without obtaining the necessary approvals from the Office of the President and the Judicial Services Commission

In the alternative, he is charged with Causing a Loss, on the basis that he dishonestly caused financial loss to the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions by accepting these unauthorised payments

He also faces a second count of Abuse of Office, alleging that he approved and received eight reimbursements totalling $47,053.03 for airfares for his wife and child, despite knowing they did not reside with him in Fiji, which is contrary to the conditions of his employment contract and in breach of procurement policy

In the alternative, he is charged with obtaining a financial advantage, for knowingly receiving payments to which he was not entitled

The third count is Causing a Loss, alleging that on or about September 16, 2022, Mr Pryde was reimbursed $7,828.56 for unused air tickets

Although the airline refunded him directly, he failed to return the amount to the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions

Ms More told the court that the decision to discontinue was because Mr Pryde had successfully managed to evade service and was beyond FICAC’s control

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Australian Minister faces questions over Fiji climate meeting costs
item.description: Australia’s Energy Minister Chris Bowen is facing renewed scrutiny after reports that taxpayers could be liable for accommodation contracts worth up to AU$4.2 million for a major climate meeting to be held in Fiji ahead of this year’s COP31 summit. According to The Australian , Australia’s Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water has reserved accommodation and meeting facili
item.content:encoded:
Australia’s Energy Minister Chris Bowen is facing renewed scrutiny after reports that taxpayers could be liable for accommodation contracts worth up to AU$4.2 million for a major climate meeting to be held in Fiji ahead of this year’s COP31 summit.

According to The Australian , Australia’s Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water has reserved accommodation and meeting facilities across three luxury resorts for a pre-COP meeting scheduled for October.

The report says the combined maximum value of the contracts is AU$4.217 million, prompting criticism from opponents who argue the spending comes as Australians continue to face rising energy bills and cost-of-living pressures.

The meeting, which Bowen will chair in his capacity as co-president of COP31, is expected to bring together international climate negotiators ahead of the main United Nations climate summit in Türkiye later this year.

The largest accommodation bookings are reportedly at the Sheraton Fiji Golf and Beach Resort and the Sofitel Fiji Resort and Spa, with additional rooms reserved at the Radisson Blu Resort Fiji.

However, the Australian Government says the final cost to taxpayers is expected to be significantly lower than the maximum contract value.

A spokesperson for the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water told The Australian that several participating countries, including Türkiye, are expected to reimburse their own accommodation costs, while unused rooms can be cancelled for refunds.

The department also said the three resorts were selected because they are among the few venues in Fiji capable of hosting an event of that scale.

It added that booking accommodation on behalf of international delegates is standard practice for major international meetings, including previous G20 and ASEAN-Australia summits.

According to the department, Australia’s support for the pre-COP meeting is part of its broader commitment to Pacific partners and efforts to strengthen regional resilience and security.

The accommodation contracts are the latest aspect of Australia’s COP31 preparations to come under political scrutiny.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji tourism tops one million annual visitors, ANZ Pacific Insight reports
item.description: Fiji’s tourism industry has reached a major milestone, with annual visitor arrivals surpassing one million for the first time, according to the latest ANZ Pacific Insight report. The report, based on Fiji Bureau of Statistics data, shows 98,522 visitors arrived in June, a 6.3 per cent increase compared to the same month last year.
item.content:encoded:
Fiji’s tourism industry has reached a major milestone, with annual visitor arrivals surpassing one million for the first time, according to the latest ANZ Pacific Insight report.

The report, based on Fiji Bureau of Statistics data, shows 98,522 visitors arrived in June, a 6.3 per cent increase compared to the same month last year.

Total arrivals for the first six months of 2026 reached 460,628, up 3.8 per cent year-on-year.

ANZ said visitor arrivals over the 12 months to June climbed to 1,003,233, marking the first time Fiji has exceeded the one-million visitor mark on a moving annual basis.

The report notes that tourism accounts for an estimated 40 per cent of Fiji’s gross domestic product, making visitor arrivals one of the country’s most important indicators of economic activity.

Australia remained Fiji’s largest tourism market in June, contributing 45,090 visitors or 45.8 per cent of total arrivals. New Zealand followed with 22,689 visitors, while the United States recorded 15,728 visitors and the strongest growth among the three largest markets, increasing 24.9 per cent compared with June last year.

Holidaymakers continued to dominate visitor arrivals, accounting for 79,810 visitors or 81 per cent of all arrivals during the month. Those visiting friends and relatives made up 7.1 per cent, while business travel represented 2.3 per cent of total arrivals.

The report says monthly visitor arrival data provides one of the most timely measures of economic performance and will continue to offer insight into the strength of Fiji’s visitor economy.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Usamate questions constitution rewrite timeline
item.description: Opposition MP Jone Usamate has questioned the Government’s plan to have a completely new Constitution ready by September, warning that the process appears to be moving away from the legal requirements set out by the Constitution and the Supreme Court. Usamate said while some may view the accelerated work as an effort to fulfil an election promise, constitutional reform must follow the law.
item.content:encoded:
Opposition MP Jone Usamate has questioned the Government’s plan to have a completely new Constitution ready by September, warning that the process appears to be moving away from the legal requirements set out by the Constitution and the Supreme Court.

Usamate said while some may view the accelerated work as an effort to fulfil an election promise, constitutional reform must follow the law.

“The announcement that a completely new constitution could be ready by September has raised serious alarm. It suggests a process that is drifting far away from the legal pathways that every government is required to follow.”

He said the Supreme Court had already clarified the process that must be followed in relation to the 2013 Constitution.

“The Constitution sets out how changes must be made, and the Supreme Court has already clarified the steps that must be followed for the 2013 Constitution. Those steps are not optional.”

Usamate also criticised recent comments by the Acting Attorney-General, saying they raised concerns that the Government may be seeking to bypass the Supreme Court’s ruling.

“The recent comments from the Acting Attorney General only deepen the concern. They create the impression that the government may be preparing to move around the very procedures the Supreme Court has already ruled on.”

He warned that if this was the Government’s intention, it would amount to more than a procedural issue.

“If that is the direction being taken, then it is not just a misunderstanding of process. It is a direct challenge to constitutional legality.”

Usamate said the country was still waiting for a revised referendum bill to be introduced after the previous proposal drew widespread criticism.

“The country is still waiting to see when a proper referendum bill will be brought to Parliament.”

He said the earlier bill contained provisions that would have restricted civic participation and public discussion and was rejected by legal experts, civil society organisations and human rights groups.

Usamate argued that replacing the Constitution should not be treated as a routine exercise.

“The government appears ready to push through an entirely new constitution, not a small amendment but a full replacement, and to do so as if it were a routine administrative task.”

He questioned whether the Acting Attorney-General fully understood the constitutional responsibilities of the office.

“The Attorney General is meant to be the independent legal voice that guides Parliament on how the law must be interpreted.”

Usamate warned that constitutional reform must remain grounded in the rule of law rather than political expediency.

“A constitution cannot be reshaped through political enthusiasm or convenience. It must be upheld through lawful, transparent, and democratic processes.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji finalising diaspora policy to ensure citizens abroad ‘keep coming back’
item.description: Fiji’s government is working on a ‘Diaspora Policy’ focused on ensuring Fijians around the globe play a key role in the country’s economic development. The country’s Minister for Foreign Affairs and External Trade Sakiasi Ditoka has just been to New Zealand, where he met with investors and the Fijian business diaspora.
item.content:encoded:
Fiji’s government is working on a ‘Diaspora Policy’ focused on ensuring Fijians around the globe play a key role in the country’s economic development.

The country’s Minister for Foreign Affairs and External Trade Sakiasi Ditoka has just been to New Zealand, where he met with investors and the Fijian business diaspora.

Ditoka told RNZ Pacific the increasing interest from the diaspora across New Zealand, Australia, the United States and Europe is positive for the island nation.

He said every effort is being made to ensure that interests are safeguarded and served for the mutual benefit of Fiji and her diaspora investors.

He also confirmed a diaspora policy is being finalised and will be sent to Cabinet for approval soon.

“It’s (diaspora policy) vital. It’s very important for Fiji that we engage with our diaspora. In fact, as we speak, the diaspora policy is being finalised. It’s going through the the final processes of being passed through Cabinet,” he told RNZ Pacific in Auckland on Saturday.

The policy is going through the development sub-committee level, which means that senior officials are now having a look at it before it is escalated to Cabinet for sign-off.

He said the policy is wide-ranging, but the central theme is “the diaspora community is very, very important to Fiji”.

Ditoka said the levels of investment members of the Fijian diaspora are putting back into the country keep increasing and help propel its economy. It includes overseas remittances, with an ever-increasing number of Fijians moving overseas for work, including sports contracts, the Recognised Seasonal Employer (RSE) scheme in New Zealand and the PALM Scheme in Australia.

The Fijian diaspora serves as the second-largest foreign exchange earner for Fiji’s economy. Personal remittances sent by the diaspora officially surpassed the $1 billion threshold between January to September 2025.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Trough to bring rain across Fiji
item.description: A trough of low pressure is expected to bring widespread rain across Fiji until Thursday, with the Fiji Meteorological Service warning that some areas could experience heavy downpours, thunderstorms and localized flooding. In its latest weather bulletin issued at 5:30am today, the National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi says occasional rain is expected over most parts of the country, with isol
item.content:encoded:
A trough of low pressure is expected to bring widespread rain across Fiji until Thursday, with the Fiji Meteorological Service warning that some areas could experience heavy downpours, thunderstorms and localized flooding.

In its latest weather bulletin issued at 5:30am today, the National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi says occasional rain is expected over most parts of the country, with isolated heavy falls forecast throughout the day.

Moderate east to northeast winds and moderate seas are also expected, while overnight conditions will remain cool.

The weather office says the trough will continue to affect the country through Thursday, with occasional showers and isolated heavy falls forecast for the Northern and Eastern divisions. The Central Division can expect some showers, while conditions in the Western Division are expected to gradually improve with only brief showers.

As the weather system approaches, the Fiji Meteorological Service is warning that periods of heavy rain and thunderstorms may reduce visibility on roads and trigger minor flooding in low-lying and flood-prone areas.

Members of the public are being urged to monitor the latest weather forecasts and warnings, exercise caution while travelling during heavy rain, avoid crossing flooded roads, rivers and creeks, and secure loose outdoor items while making the necessary preparations should weather conditions worsen.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji universities criticised for ignoring coup impacts
item.description: Fiji’s universities have been criticised for failing to study the long-term effects of the country’s repeated political upheavals on the public service and governance, with the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission hearing that a major gap in research has left important national lessons undocumented. Academic Prof Vijay Naidu told the Commission it was “a great shame” that higher education inst
item.content:encoded:
Fiji’s universities have been criticised for failing to study the long-term effects of the country’s repeated political upheavals on the public service and governance, with the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission hearing that a major gap in research has left important national lessons undocumented.

Academic Prof Vijay Naidu told the Commission it was “a great shame” that higher education institutions had not examined how cycles of political instability had affected the quality and performance of Fiji’s public sector.

“It’s a great shame that the universities in Fiji have failed to look at the impacts of these cycles of political instability on the nature, the quality and the function of the public sector.”

Dr Naidu drew particular attention to the University of the South Pacific, saying its once-prominent governance programme had disappeared despite the country’s history of political crises.

“The governance programme was a flagship programme of the university in the early 2000s… Well, the governance programme is gone. It’s finished.”

He questioned what that said about the role of universities in serving Fiji and the wider Pacific.

“What does it tell you about universities serving Fiji and the region when governance is such an important issue?”

Dr Naidu said strengthening democratic institutions required sustained effort and could not be achieved overnight.

“The general democratic institutions and the checks and balances associated with them, together with a supportive democratic culture, have to be built incrementally.”

He warned that every military coup had reversed progress made in democratic governance and institution building.

“The analogy of switching the light on and off doesn’t work. Democracy doesn’t work that way. Each time we have had a military coup, it’s actually taken us backwards.”

Referring to earlier evidence presented by Professor Graham Hassall, Dr Naidu said the damage extended beyond the public service to the broader confidence people had in national institutions.

“The wider implication is that there is a loss of confidence.”

He said understanding the long-term consequences of political instability was essential if Fiji was to rebuild stronger democratic institutions and avoid repeating the mistakes of the past.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
